curriculum_source: CFA Program 2027 Level III, Volume 5 (Ethical and Professional Standards), Learning Module 1: Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional Conduct

reading.reading_name: Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional Conduct

drills.los_text: describe the structure of the CFA Institute Professional Conduct Program and the disciplinary review process for the enforcement of the CFA Institute Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional Conduct
drills.reading_name: Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional Conduct

questions.stem: *Structure of CFA Institute Professional Conduct enforcement* *most likely*:

options.A: CFA Institute Board of Governors maintains oversight and responsibility for Professional Conduct (PC) group, which in conjunction with Disciplinary Review Committee (DRC), is responsible for enforcement of Code and Standards.
options.B: Professional Conduct investigations are administered solely by an independent external law firm retained by the Board, with no standing internal PC staff.
options.C: Disciplinary authority rests exclusively with the Disciplinary Review Committee, which operates independently of, and without oversight from, the Board of Governors.

rationales.A:
Full statement: CFA Institute Board of Governors maintains oversight and responsibility for Professional Conduct (PC) group, which in conjunction with Disciplinary Review Committee (DRC), is responsible for enforcement of Code and Standards; PC staff investigates allegations of misconduct by members and candidates; DRC is volunteer committee of CFA charterholders who serve on hearing panels to review misconduct allegations and impose sanctions for violations of Code and Standards; DRC also partners with PC staff to establish and review professional conduct policies.

Correct. Per the reading: 'All CFA Institute members and candidates for the CFA and CIPM® designations are required to comply with the Code and Standards. The CFA Institute Board of Governors maintains oversight and responsibility for the Professional Conduct (PC) group, which, in conjunction with the Disciplinary Review Committee (DRC), is responsible for enforcement of the Code and Standards. The PC staff investigates allegations of misconduct by members and candidates. The DRC is a volunteer committee of CFA charterholders who serve on hearing panels to review misconduct allegations and impose sanctions for violations of the Code and Standards. The DRC also partners with PC staff to establish and review professional conduct policies.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. There is a standing internal Professional Conduct (PC) group; enforcement is not outsourced to external counsel.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The Board of Governors maintains oversight and responsibility; the DRC does not operate free of the Board.

questions.stem: *Basic structure for enforcing Code and Standards* *most likely*:

options.A: The Standards of Practice Handbook itself constitutes the enforcement mechanism for the Code and Standards.
options.B: CFA Institute Bylaws and Rules of Procedure (Rules of Procedure) form the basic structure for enforcing the Code and Standards.
options.C: Enforcement authority derives from national securities regulators acting on CFA Institute's behalf.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The Handbook provides guidance; the Bylaws and Rules of Procedure form the enforcement structure.
rationales.B:
Full statement: CFA Institute Bylaws and Rules of Procedure (Rules of Procedure) form the basic structure for enforcing the Code and Standards; establish procedural framework for investigations, hearings, sanctions and other disciplinary matters.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The CFA Institute Bylaws and Rules of Procedure (Rules of Procedure) form the basic structure for enforcing the Code and Standards.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Enforcement is a private professional-conduct process of CFA Institute, not delegated government regulation.

questions.stem: *Four sources of Professional Conduct investigations* *most likely*:

options.A: Investigations may be initiated only when a member self-discloses on the annual Professional Conduct Statement.
options.B: Anonymous or written complaints are disregarded, and media reports cannot trigger an inquiry.
options.C: (1) members and candidates must self-disclose on annual Professional Conduct Statement all matters that question their professional conduct (e.g., involvement in civil litigation or criminal investigation, being subject of written complaint).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Self-disclosure is one of four sources; written complaints, public/media sources, and exam-related monitoring also initiate inquiries.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Written complaints and media or other public sources are explicitly recognized triggers of inquiry.
rationales.C:
Full statement: (1) members and candidates must self-disclose on annual Professional Conduct Statement all matters that question their professional conduct (e.g., involvement in civil litigation or criminal investigation, being subject of written complaint); (2) written complaints received by PC staff; (3) CFA Institute staff may become aware of questionable conduct through media, regulatory notices, or another public source; (4) CFA Institute works with testing centers, conducts analyses of scores and exam materials after exams, and monitors online and social media to detect potential misconduct including disclosure of confidential exam information.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Professional conduct investigations arise from a number of sources. First, members and candidates must self-disclose on the annual Professional Conduct Statement all matters that question their professional conduct, such as involvement in civil litigation or a criminal investigation or being the subject of a written complaint. Second, written complaints received by PC staff can bring about an investigation. Third, CFA Institute staff may become aware of questionable conduct by a member or candidate through the media, regulatory notices, or another public source. Lastly, CFA Institute works with testing centers, conducts analyses of scores and exam materials after exams, and monitors online and social media to detect potential misconduct, including disclosure of confidential exam information.'

questions.stem: *Annual Professional Conduct Statement* *most likely* purpose:

options.A: members and candidates MUST self-disclose on annual Professional Conduct Statement all matters that question their professional conduct, such as involvement in civil litigation or criminal investigation or being subject of written complaint.
options.B: Completion of the annual Professional Conduct Statement is voluntary and serves only as a reminder of the Code.
options.C: The Statement requires disclosure only of criminal convictions, not civil litigation or written complaints.

rationales.A:
Full statement: members and candidates MUST self-disclose on annual Professional Conduct Statement all matters that question their professional conduct, such as involvement in civil litigation or criminal investigation or being subject of written complaint; is one of four primary sources of professional conduct investigations.

Correct. Per the reading: 'members and candidates must self-disclose on the annual Professional Conduct Statement all matters that question their professional conduct, such as involvement in civil litigation or a criminal investigation or being the subject of a written complaint.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Self-disclosure on the annual Statement is mandatory for members and candidates.
rationales.C: Incorrect. It covers all matters questioning professional conduct, including civil litigation and written complaints.

questions.stem: *Media and regulatory notices as source* *most likely*:

options.A: Media reports are not a recognized source of Professional Conduct inquiries because they are unverified.
options.B: CFA Institute staff may become aware of questionable conduct by a member or candidate through the media, regulatory notices, or another public source.
options.C: Only regulator referrals accompanied by formal findings can initiate an inquiry.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Staff may become aware of questionable conduct through media, regulatory notices, or other public sources.
rationales.B:
Full statement: CFA Institute staff may become aware of questionable conduct by a member or candidate through the media, regulatory notices, or another public source; this is one of four sources of professional conduct investigations — inquiries CAN be initiated in response to information provided by the media.

Correct. Per the reading: 'CFA Institute staff may become aware of questionable conduct by a member or candidate through the media, regulatory notices, or another public source.' Also from Solution 1: 'Professional Conduct inquiries can be initiated in response to information provided by the media.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Public sources need not be regulator referrals with findings; media reports alone can prompt an inquiry.

questions.stem: *Testing center monitoring* *most likely*:

options.A: Exam monitoring ends when the testing session closes; post-exam score and material analyses are not conducted.
options.B: CFA Institute does not monitor online or social media activity for exam-related misconduct.
options.C: CFA Institute works with testing centers, conducts analyses of scores and exam materials after exams, and monitors online and social media to detect potential misconduct, including disclosure of confidential exam information.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Analyses of scores and exam materials are performed after exams as part of misconduct detection.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Online and social media monitoring is an explicit component of exam-integrity efforts.
rationales.C:
Full statement: CFA Institute works with testing centers, conducts analyses of scores and exam materials after exams, and monitors online and social media to detect potential misconduct, including disclosure of confidential exam information; this represents fourth source of professional conduct investigations.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Lastly, CFA Institute works with testing centers, conducts analyses of scores and exam materials after exams, and monitors online and social media to detect potential misconduct, including disclosure of confidential exam information.'

questions.stem: *What PC staff investigation may include* *most likely*:

options.A: when inquiry is initiated, PC staff conducts investigation that may include: requesting written explanation from member or candidate.
options.B: PC staff may not contact the member during an investigation and must rely solely on third-party records.
options.C: An investigation is limited to a review of public filings; written explanations cannot be requested.

rationales.A:
Full statement: when inquiry is initiated, PC staff conducts investigation that may include: requesting written explanation from member or candidate; interviewing the member or candidate, complaining parties, and third parties; and collecting documents and records relevant to investigation.

Correct. Per the reading: 'When an inquiry is initiated, the PC staff conducts an investigation that may include requesting a written explanation from the member or candidate; interviewing the member or candidate, complaining parties, and third parties; and collecting documents and records relevant to the investigation.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Investigations may include requesting a written explanation directly from the member or candidate.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Requesting the member's written explanation (and supporting interviews/records) is a core investigative step.

questions.stem: *PC staff resolution options* after review *most likely*:

options.A: single option.
options.B: upon reviewing material obtained during investigation, PC staff may: (1) conclude inquiry with no disciplinary sanction.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Three resolution options.
rationales.B:
Full statement: upon reviewing material obtained during investigation, PC staff may: (1) conclude inquiry with no disciplinary sanction; (2) issue cautionary letter; or (3) continue proceedings to discipline the member or candidate; if PC staff believes violation of Code and Standards has occurred, member or candidate has opportunity to reject or accept any charges and proposed sanction.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Upon reviewing the material obtained during the investigation, the PC staff may conclude the inquiry with no disciplinary sanction, issue a cautionary letter, or continue proceedings to discipline the member or candidate. If the PC staff believes a violation of the Code and Standards has occurred, the member or candidate has the opportunity to reject or accept any charges and the proposed sanction.'
rationales.C: Three options.

questions.stem: *DRC panel process* when member rejects charges *most likely*:

options.A: If the member rejects the charges, the matter is automatically referred to civil courts for resolution.
options.B: Rejecting a proposed sanction results in automatic summary suspension without further review.
options.C: if member or candidate does NOT accept charges and proposed sanction, matter is referred to a PANEL composed of DRC members.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Enforcement is a private professional process; rejection refers the matter to a hearing panel of DRC members.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Rejection triggers panel review, not automatic sanction.
rationales.C:
Full statement: if member or candidate does NOT accept charges and proposed sanction, matter is referred to a PANEL composed of DRC members; DRC panels evaluate materials and presentations from PC staff and from member or candidate; in contested matters, DRC panel's task is to determine whether member or candidate violated Code and Standards or testing policies and, if so, what sanction should be imposed; DRC panels also review matters where members/candidates agree to PC's proposed charges and sanction to ensure findings are reasonable.

Correct. Per the reading: 'If the member or candidate does not accept the charges and proposed sanction, the matter is referred to a panel composed of DRC members. DRC panels evaluate materials and presentations from PC staff and from the member or candidate. In contested matters, the DRC panel's task is to determine whether the member or candidate violated the Code and Standards or testing policies and, if so, what sanction should be imposed. DRC panels also review matters where members and candidates agree to PC's proposed charges and sanction to ensure that the findings are reasonable.'

questions.stem: *Sanctions available to CFA Institute* *most likely*:

options.A: sanctions imposed by CFA Institute may have significant consequences.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: sanctions imposed by CFA Institute may have significant consequences; sanctions include: public censure; suspension of membership and use of CFA designation; and revocation of membership and the right to use CFA designation; candidates enrolled in CFA Program who have violated Code and Standards or testing policies may be suspended or prohibited from further participation in CFA Program.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Sanctions imposed by CFA Institute may have significant consequences; sanctions include public censure, suspension of membership and use of the CFA designation, and revocation of membership and the right to use the CFA designation. Candidates enrolled in the CFA Program who have violated the Code and Standards or testing policies may be suspended or prohibited from further participation in the CFA Program.'
rationales.B: Censure / suspension / revocation.
rationales.C: Sanctions.

questions.stem: *Adoption of Code and Standards by firms* *most likely*:

options.A: prohibited.
options.B: Code and Standards apply to individual members of CFA Institute and candidates for CFA and CIPM designations.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Firms encouraged to adopt.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Code and Standards apply to individual members of CFA Institute and candidates for CFA and CIPM designations; CFA Institute does ENCOURAGE firms to adopt Code and Standards as part of their code of ethics; those who claim compliance should fully understand requirements of each principle; once party — nonmember or firm — ensures its code of ethics meets principles of Code and Standards, that party should make specific statement whenever claiming compliance: '[Insert name of party] claims compliance with the CFA Institute Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional Conduct. This claim has not been verified by CFA Institute.'

Correct. Per the reading: 'The Code and Standards apply to individual members of CFA Institute and candidates for the CFA and CIPM designations. CFA Institute does encourage firms to adopt the Code and Standards, however, as part of their code of ethics. Those who claim compliance should fully understand the requirements of each principle of the Code and Standards. Once a party — nonmember or firm — ensures its code of ethics meets the principles of the Code and Standards, that party should make the following statement whenever claiming compliance: [Insert name of party] claims compliance with the CFA Institute Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional Conduct. This claim has not been verified by CFA Institute.'
rationales.C: Adoption + statement.

questions.stem: *CFA Institute Asset Manager Code* *most likely*:

options.A: The Asset Manager Code applies only to individual charterholders and cannot be adopted at the firm level.
options.B: Compliance with the Asset Manager Code is mandatory for any firm that employs CFA charterholders.
options.C: CFA Institute has also published CFA Institute Asset Manager Code designed in part to help asset managers comply with regulations mandating codes of ethics for investment advisers.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The Asset Manager Code is designed for adoption by firms (asset managers), not by individuals.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Adoption of the Asset Manager Code is voluntary; employing charterholders does not make it mandatory.
rationales.C:
Full statement: CFA Institute has also published CFA Institute Asset Manager Code designed in part to help asset managers comply with regulations mandating codes of ethics for investment advisers; whereas Code and Standards are aimed at individual investment professionals who are members of CFA Institute or candidates for CFA Institute designations, Asset Manager Code applies to FIRMS; provides specific practical guidelines for asset managers in six areas: loyalty to clients, investment process, trading, compliance, performance, and disclosure.

Correct. Per the reading: 'CFA Institute has also published the CFA Institute Asset Manager Code, which is designed, in part, to help asset managers comply with regulations mandating codes of ethics for investment advisers. Whereas the Code and Standards are aimed at individual investment professionals who are members of CFA Institute or candidates for CFA Institute designations, the Asset Manager Code applies to firms. The Asset Manager Code provides specific, practical guidelines for asset managers in six areas: loyalty to clients, the investment process, trading, compliance, performance, and disclosure.'

questions.stem: *Standards of Practice Council (SPC)* *most likely*:

options.A: group consisting of CFA charterholder volunteers from many different markets.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: group consisting of CFA charterholder volunteers from many different markets; charged with maintaining and interpreting Code and Standards and ensuring they are effective; SPC draws its membership from broad spectrum of organizations in securities field including brokers, investment advisers, banks, and insurance companies; continually evaluates Code and Standards and guidance in Handbook to ensure they are representative of high standards of professional conduct, relevant to changing nature of investment profession, globally applicable, and sufficiently comprehensive, practical, and specific.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The CFA Institute Standards of Practice Council (SPC), a group consisting of CFA charterholder volunteers from many different markets, is charged with maintaining and interpreting the Code and Standards and ensuring that they are effective. The SPC draws its membership from a broad spectrum of organizations in the securities field, including brokers, investment advisers, banks, and insurance companies. The SPC continually evaluates the Code and Standards, as well as the guidance in the Handbook, to ensure that they are representative of high standards of professional conduct, relevant to the changing nature of the investment profession, globally applicable, and sufficiently comprehensive, practical, and specific.'
rationales.B: SPC role.
rationales.C: SPC.

questions.stem: *Standards of Practice Handbook* purpose *most likely*:

options.A: no purpose.
options.B: provides guidance to people who grapple with real ethical dilemmas in investment profession on daily basis.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Handbook purpose.
rationales.B:
Full statement: provides guidance to people who grapple with real ethical dilemmas in investment profession on daily basis; addresses professional intersection where theory meets practice and where concept of ethical behavior crosses from abstract to concrete; written for diverse global audience of CFA Institute members and investment professionals who are navigating ambiguous ethical situations and seeking to understand nature of their responsibilities to each other, to existing and potential clients, and to broader financial markets.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The Standards of Practice Handbook (Handbook) provides guidance to the people who grapple with real ethical dilemmas in the investment profession on a daily basis; the Handbook addresses the professional intersection where theory meets practice and where the concept of ethical behavior crosses from the abstract to the concrete. The Handbook is written for a diverse global audience of CFA Institute members and investment professionals who are navigating ambiguous ethical situations and seeking to understand the nature of their responsibilities to each other, to existing and potential clients, and to the broader financial markets.'
rationales.C: Handbook.

questions.stem: *Ethics definition* per the reading *most likely*:

options.A: Ethics is defined as the body of laws and regulations enacted by governments to compel minimum standards of conduct.
options.B: Ethics is defined as the study of maximizing client wealth subject to prevailing legal constraints.
options.C: ethics can be defined as set of principles or rules of conduct that provide guidance for our behavior when it affects others.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Ethics is broader than law; legal compliance is neither necessary nor sufficient for ethical conduct.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Ethics concerns principles or rules of conduct affecting others, not wealth maximization within legal bounds.
rationales.C:
Full statement: ethics can be defined as set of principles or rules of conduct that provide guidance for our behavior when it affects others; widely acknowledged fundamental ethical principles include honesty, fairness, diligence, and care and respect for others; ethical conduct follows those principles and balances self-interest with both direct and indirect consequences of that behavior for other people.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Ethics can be defined as a set of principles or rules of conduct that provide guidance for our behavior when it affects others. Widely acknowledged fundamental ethical principles include honesty, fairness, diligence, and care and respect for others. Ethical conduct follows those principles and balances self-interest with both the direct and the indirect consequences of that behavior for other people.'

questions.stem: *Why compliance with regulation is insufficient* *most likely*:

options.A: compliance with regulation alone is insufficient to fully earn investor trust.
options.B: sufficient.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: compliance with regulation alone is insufficient to fully earn investor trust; individuals and firms must develop 'culture of integrity' that permeates all levels of operations and promotes ethical principles of stewardship of investor assets and working in best interests of clients, above and beyond strict compliance with law; strong ethical culture that helps honest ethical people engage in ethical behavior will foster trust of investors, lead to robust global capital markets, and ultimately benefit society; inevitably, some individuals will succeed at circumventing regulatory rules for personal gain; only application of strong ethical principles at both individual and firm level will limit abuses.

Correct. Per the reading: 'compliance with regulation alone is insufficient to fully earn investor trust. Individuals and firms must develop a "culture of integrity" that permeates all levels of operations and promotes the ethical principles of stewardship of investor assets and working in the best interests of clients, above and beyond strict compliance with the law. A strong ethical culture that helps honest, ethical people engage in ethical behavior will foster the trust of investors, lead to robust global capital markets, and ultimately benefit society... Inevitably, some individuals will succeed at circumventing the regulatory rules for their personal gain. Only the application of strong ethical principles, at both the individual level and the firm level, will limit abuses.'
rationales.B: Culture of integrity.
rationales.C: Beyond compliance.

questions.stem: *Applying an ethical framework* *most likely*:

options.A: Strict adherence to laws and regulations alone is sufficient to guarantee ethical behavior, leaving no role for individual judgment.
options.B: laws, regulations, professional standards, and codes of ethics can guide ethical behavior, but individual judgment is critical ingredient in making principled choices.
options.C: Codes of ethics are designed to replace individual judgment with prescriptive rules covering every possible situation.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Laws and codes guide behavior, but individual judgment is the critical ingredient in principled choices.
rationales.B:
Full statement: laws, regulations, professional standards, and codes of ethics can guide ethical behavior, but individual judgment is critical ingredient in making principled choices; when faced with ethical dilemma, individuals must have well-developed set of principles — otherwise thought processes can lead to equivocation and indecision or fraudulent conduct and destruction of public trust; establishing ethical framework for internal thought process prior to deciding to act is crucial step in engaging in ethical conduct; ethical decision-making framework should provide investment professionals with tool for following principles of firm's code of ethics.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Laws, regulations, professional standards, and codes of ethics can guide ethical behavior, but individual judgment is a critical ingredient in making principled choices and engaging in appropriate conduct. When faced with an ethical dilemma, individuals must have a well-developed set of principles; otherwise, their thought processes can lead to, at best, equivocation and indecision and, at worst, fraudulent conduct and destruction of the public trust. Establishing an ethical framework for an internal thought process prior to deciding to act is a crucial step in engaging in ethical conduct.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. No code anticipates every situation; frameworks support rather than replace judgment.

questions.stem: *Culture of integrity at firm level* *most likely*:

options.A: Ethical conduct is purely a matter of individual character, so firm-level culture has little influence on behavior.
options.B: A culture of integrity is established primarily through one-time compliance training delivered at hiring.
options.C: investment professional's natural desire to 'do the right thing' must be reinforced by building culture of integrity in workplace.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The reading stresses that the individual desire to do right must be reinforced by a workplace culture of integrity.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Culture is built through ongoing reinforcement by leadership and practice, not a single onboarding session.
rationales.C:
Full statement: investment professional's natural desire to 'do the right thing' must be reinforced by building culture of integrity in workplace; development, maintenance, and demonstration of strong culture of integrity within firm by senior management may be single most important factor in promoting ethical behavior among firm's employees; adopting code of ethics that clearly lays out ethical principles that guide thought processes and conduct firm expects from its employees is critical first step — but code of ethics while necessary is insufficient; need to exercise ethical decision-making skills to develop 'muscle memory' necessary for fundamentally ethical people to make good decisions despite reality of conflicts of interest.

Correct. Per the reading: 'An investment professional's natural desire to "do the right thing" must be reinforced by building a culture of integrity in the workplace. Development, maintenance, and demonstration of a strong culture of integrity within the firm by senior management may be the single most important factor in promoting ethical behavior among the firm's employees. Adopting a code of ethics that clearly lays out the ethical principles that guide the thought processes and conduct the firm expects from its employees is a critical first step. But a code of ethics, while necessary, is insufficient. Simply nurturing an inclination to do right is no match for the multitude of daily decisions that investment managers make. We need to exercise ethical decision-making skills to develop the "muscle memory" necessary for fundamentally ethical people to make good decisions despite the reality of conflicts of interest.'

drills.los_text: explain the ethical responsibilities required by the Code and Standards, including the subsections of each standard
drills.reading_name: Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional Conduct

questions.stem: *Preamble to Code and Standards* *most likely* states:

options.A: Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional Conduct are fundamental to values of CFA Institute and essential to achieving its mission.
options.B: The Preamble states that the Standards are aspirational goals that members should strive toward but need not attain.
options.C: The Preamble limits the Code's applicability to members employed by investment management firms.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional Conduct are fundamental to values of CFA Institute and essential to achieving its mission; since their creation in 1960s, Code and Standards have promoted integrity of CFA Institute members and served as model for measuring ethics of investment professionals globally, regardless of job function, cultural differences, or local laws and regulations; all CFA Institute members and CFA candidates have personal responsibility to embrace and uphold provisions of Code and Standards and are encouraged to notify their employer of this responsibility; violations may result in disciplinary sanctions by CFA Institute including revocation of membership, revocation of candidacy in CFA Program, and revocation of right to use CFA designation.

Correct. Per the reading Preamble: 'The CFA Institute Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional Conduct are fundamental to the values of CFA Institute and essential to achieving its mission... Since their creation in the 1960s, the Code and Standards have promoted the integrity of CFA Institute members and served as a model for measuring the ethics of investment professionals globally, regardless of job function, cultural differences, or local laws and regulations. All CFA Institute members (including holders of the Chartered Financial Analyst® [CFA] designation) and CFA candidates have the personal responsibility to embrace and uphold the provisions of the Code and Standards and are encouraged to notify their employer of this responsibility. Violations may result in disciplinary sanctions by CFA Institute. Sanctions can include revocation of membership, revocation of candidacy in the CFA Program, and revocation of the right to use the CFA designation.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The Code and Standards are binding on members and candidates, not merely aspirational.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The Code applies to all members and candidates regardless of employer type.

questions.stem: *Code of Ethics first principle* *most likely* requires Members and Candidates to:

options.A: The first principle requires placing the employer's interests above those of clients and market integrity.
options.B: act with integrity, competence, diligence, and respect and in an ethical manner with the public, clients, prospective clients, employers, employees, colleagues in the investment profession, and other participants in the global capital markets.
options.C: The first principle addresses only conduct toward clients, excluding colleagues and the public.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The Code requires integrity and ethical conduct toward the public, clients, employers, employees, and colleagues; client interests and market integrity come before personal or employer advantage.
rationales.B: Correct. Per the Code of Ethics first principle: 'Act with integrity, competence, diligence, and respect and in an ethical manner with the public, clients, prospective clients, employers, employees, colleagues in the investment profession, and other participants in the global capital markets.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The first principle explicitly names the public, clients, prospects, employers, employees, and colleagues.

questions.stem: *Code of Ethics second principle* *most likely* requires:

options.A: no principle.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: place the integrity of the investment profession and the interests of clients above their own personal interests.

rationales.A: Interests above own.
rationales.B: Priority.
rationales.C: Correct. Per the Code of Ethics second principle: 'Place the integrity of the investment profession and the interests of clients above their own personal interests.'

questions.stem: *Code of Ethics third principle* *most likely* requires:

options.A: use reasonable care and exercise independent professional judgment when conducting investment analysis, making investment recommendations, taking investment actions, and engaging in other professional activities.
options.B: The third principle requires maximizing returns for clients regardless of the level of care exercised.
options.C: Independent professional judgment may be subordinated to the consensus view of the firm's research committee.

rationales.A: Correct. Per the Code of Ethics third principle: 'Use reasonable care and exercise independent professional judgment when conducting investment analysis, making investment recommendations, taking investment actions, and engaging in other professional activities.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The principle concerns reasonable care and independent judgment, not a return-maximization guarantee.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Judgment must remain independent; committee consensus does not replace it.

questions.stem: *Code of Ethics fourth principle* *most likely* requires:

options.A: The fourth principle concerns only one's own practice and imposes no duty regarding the conduct of others.
options.B: practice and encourage others to practice in a professional and ethical manner that will reflect credit on themselves and the profession.
options.C: The fourth principle requires reporting all observed violations to securities regulators.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Members must also ENCOURAGE OTHERS to practice professionally and ethically.
rationales.B: Correct. Per the Code of Ethics fourth principle: 'Practice and encourage others to practice in a professional and ethical manner that will reflect credit on themselves and the profession.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The principle concerns practicing and encouraging ethical practice, not a regulatory reporting mandate.

questions.stem: *Code of Ethics fifth principle* *most likely* requires:

options.A: no principle.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: promote the integrity and viability of the global capital markets for the ultimate benefit of society.

rationales.A: Integrity for society.
rationales.B: Society benefit.
rationales.C: Correct. Per the Code of Ethics fifth principle: 'Promote the integrity and viability of the global capital markets for the ultimate benefit of society.' Note per Solution 2: this reflects the role that capital markets have in society as a whole — versions omitting 'for the ultimate benefit of society' are incomplete.

questions.stem: *Code of Ethics sixth principle* *most likely* requires:

options.A: maintain and improve their professional competence and strive to maintain and improve the competence of other investment professionals.
options.B: The sixth principle concerns maintaining one's own competence only, with no reference to other professionals.
options.C: The sixth principle requires attaining a specified number of continuing education credits annually.

rationales.A: Correct. Per the Code of Ethics sixth principle: 'Maintain and improve their professional competence and strive to maintain and improve the competence of other investment professionals.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Members must also strive to maintain and improve the competence of OTHER investment professionals.
rationales.C: Incorrect. No specific credit count is mandated by the Code's principles.

questions.stem: *Standard I: Professionalism* *most likely* has how many subsections and covers:

options.A: Standard I contains four subsections, ending with I(D) Misconduct.
options.B: five subsections — I(A) Knowledge of the Law, I(B) Independence and Objectivity, I(C) Misrepresentation, I(D) Misconduct, I(E) Competence.
options.C: Standard I includes a subsection on Fair Dealing.

rationales.A: Incorrect. There are five subsections; I(E) Competence completes the list.
rationales.B: Correct. Per the Standards of Professional Conduct: 'i. PROFESSIONALISM: A. Knowledge of the Law; B. Independence and Objectivity; C. Misrepresentation; D. Misconduct; E. Competence.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Fair Dealing is Standard III(B), under Duties to Clients.

questions.stem: *Standard I(A) Knowledge of the Law* *most likely* requires:

options.A: Members and candidates need only comply with the local law of the jurisdiction where they work and may disregard the Code and Standards wherever local law is silent.
options.B: Compliance requires members to acquire detailed expert knowledge of the laws of every government, regulatory organization, and agency to which they could conceivably be subject.
options.C: understand and comply with all applicable laws, rules, and regulations (including CFA Institute Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional Conduct) of any government, regulatory organization, licensing agency, or professional association governing their professional activities.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Where law is less strict or silent, the Code and Standards govern.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The duty is to UNDERSTAND and comply with applicable law; expert mastery of all laws is not required.
rationales.C:
Full statement: understand and comply with all applicable laws, rules, and regulations (including CFA Institute Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional Conduct) of any government, regulatory organization, licensing agency, or professional association governing their professional activities; in event of conflict, must comply with the MORE STRICT law, rule, or regulation; must NOT knowingly participate or assist in and must DISSOCIATE from any violation of such laws, rules, or regulations.

Correct. Per Standard I(A): 'Members and Candidates must understand and comply with all applicable laws, rules, and regulations (including the CFA Institute Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional Conduct) of any government, regulatory organization, licensing agency, or professional association governing their professional activities. In the event of conflict, Members and Candidates must comply with the more strict law, rule, or regulation. Members and Candidates must not knowingly participate or assist in and must dissociate from any violation of such laws, rules, or regulations.'

questions.stem: *Standard I(B) Independence and Objectivity* *most likely* requires:

options.A: use reasonable care and judgment to achieve and maintain independence and objectivity in their professional activities.
options.B: no requirement.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: use reasonable care and judgment to achieve and maintain independence and objectivity in their professional activities; must NOT offer, solicit, or accept any gift, benefit, compensation, or consideration that reasonably could be expected to compromise their own or another's independence and objectivity.

Correct. Per Standard I(B): 'Members and Candidates must use reasonable care and judgment to achieve and maintain independence and objectivity in their professional activities. Members and Candidates must not offer, solicit, or accept any gift, benefit, compensation, or consideration that reasonably could be expected to compromise their own or another's independence and objectivity.'
rationales.B: No compromising gifts.
rationales.C: Independence + gifts.

questions.stem: *Standard I(C) Misrepresentation* *most likely* requires:

options.A: Standard I(C) prohibits only intentional misrepresentations made in written research reports.
options.B: must NOT knowingly make any misrepresentations relating to investment analysis, recommendations, actions, or other professional activities.
options.C: Standard I(C) permits misrepresentations provided they are corrected within a reasonable period.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The prohibition covers any knowing misrepresentation across analysis, recommendations, actions, or other professional activities — not only written reports.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Standard I(C): 'Members and Candidates must not knowingly make any misrepresentations relating to investment analysis, recommendations, actions, or other professional activities.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Knowing misrepresentation is prohibited outright; later correction does not authorize it.

questions.stem: *Standard I(D) Misconduct* *most likely* requires:

options.A: Standard I(D) applies only to conduct occurring on the employer's premises during business hours.
options.B: Standard I(D) is limited to conduct resulting in criminal convictions for fraud.
options.C: must NOT engage in any professional conduct involving dishonesty, fraud, or deceit or commit any act that reflects adversely on their professional reputation, integrity, or competence.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Misconduct covers professional conduct involving dishonesty, fraud, or deceit wherever it occurs.
rationales.B: Incorrect. No conviction is required; acts reflecting adversely on professional reputation, integrity, or competence are covered.
rationales.C: Correct. Per Standard I(D): 'Members and Candidates must not engage in any professional conduct involving dishonesty, fraud, or deceit or commit any act that reflects adversely on their professional reputation, integrity, or competence.'

questions.stem: *Standard I(E) Competence* *most likely* requires:

options.A: must act with and maintain the competence necessary to fulfill their professional responsibilities.
options.B: no requirement.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Correct. Per Standard I(E): 'Members and Candidates must act with and maintain the competence necessary to fulfill their professional responsibilities.'
rationales.B: Competence maintenance.
rationales.C: Competence.

questions.stem: *Standard II: Integrity of Capital Markets* *most likely* subsections:

options.A: no framework.
options.B: two subsections — II(A) Material Nonpublic Information and II(B) Market Manipulation.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: II(A) and II(B).
rationales.B: Correct. Per the Standards of Professional Conduct: 'ii. INTEGRITY OF CAPITAL MARKETS: A. Material Nonpublic Information; B. Market Manipulation.'
rationales.C: II(A)-(B).

questions.stem: *Standard II(A) Material Nonpublic Information* *most likely* requires:

options.A: Members possessing material nonpublic information may act on it provided they disclose the trade to their employer.
options.B: The prohibition applies only to information obtained directly from company insiders.
options.C: Members and Candidates who possess material nonpublic information that could affect the value of an investment must NOT act or cause others to act on the information.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Acting (or causing others to act) on MNPI is prohibited; employer disclosure does not cure it.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The source of the information is irrelevant; possession of MNPI triggers the prohibition.
rationales.C: Correct. Per Standard II(A): 'Members and Candidates who possess material nonpublic information that could affect the value of an investment must not act or cause others to act on the information.' Per Solution 5: 'inappropriately causing others to act' violates Standard II(A).

questions.stem: *Standard II(B) Market Manipulation* *most likely* requires:

options.A: must NOT engage in practices that distort prices or artificially inflate trading volume with the intent to mislead market participants.
options.B: Standard II(B) prohibits all trading strategies that move market prices, regardless of intent.
options.C: Standard II(B) applies only to spreading false rumors, not to transaction-based manipulation.

rationales.A: Correct. Per Standard II(B): 'Members and Candidates must not engage in practices that distort prices or artificially inflate trading volume with the intent to mislead market participants.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Intent to mislead is the key element; legitimate trading that moves prices is not manipulation.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Both information-based and transaction-based manipulation (distorting prices or inflating volume) are covered.

questions.stem: *Standard III: Duties to Clients* *most likely* subsections:

options.A: no framework.
options.B: five subsections — III(A) Loyalty, Prudence, and Care.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Five subsections.
rationales.B:
Full statement: five subsections — III(A) Loyalty, Prudence, and Care; III(B) Fair Dealing; III(C) Suitability; III(D) Performance Presentation; III(E) Preservation of Confidentiality.

Correct. Per the Standards of Professional Conduct: 'iii. DUTIES TO CLIENTS: A. Loyalty, Prudence, and Care; B. Fair Dealing; C. Suitability; D. Performance Presentation; E. Preservation of Confidentiality.'
rationales.C: III(A)-(E).

questions.stem: *Standard III(A) Loyalty, Prudence, and Care* *most likely* requires:

options.A: no requirement.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: have a duty of loyalty to their clients and must act with reasonable care and exercise prudent judgment.

rationales.A: Clients first.
rationales.B: Loyalty.
rationales.C:
Full statement: have a duty of loyalty to their clients and must act with reasonable care and exercise prudent judgment; must act for the benefit of their clients and place their clients' interests before their employer's or their own interests.

Correct. Per Standard III(A): 'Members and Candidates have a duty of loyalty to their clients and must act with reasonable care and exercise prudent judgment. Members and Candidates must act for the benefit of their clients and place their clients' interests before their employer's or their own interests.'

questions.stem: *Standard III(B) Fair Dealing* *most likely* requires:

options.A: must deal fairly and objectively with ALL clients when providing investment analysis, making investment recommendations, taking investment action, or engaging in other professional activities.
options.B: Fair dealing requires that all clients be treated equally, receiving identical recommendations and allocations.
options.C: Standard III(B) applies when making recommendations but not when taking investment action.

rationales.A: Correct. Per Standard III(B): 'Members and Candidates must deal fairly and objectively with all clients when providing investment analysis, making investment recommendations, taking investment action, or engaging in other professional activities.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The requirement is fair and objective dealing, not identical treatment; differing service levels are permissible if disclosed and not disadvantaging.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Fair dealing expressly covers analysis, recommendations, investment action, and other professional activities.

questions.stem: *Standard III(C) Suitability requirements (advisory relationship)* *most likely*:

options.A: In an advisory relationship, the suitability inquiry applies only to fully discretionary accounts; nondiscretionary advisory clients require no assessment of experience, objectives, or constraints.
options.B: when in advisory relationship with client, must: (a) make reasonable inquiry into client's or prospective client's investment experience, risk and return objectives, and financial constraints PRIOR to making any investment recommendation or taking investment action and must REASSESS and update this information REGULARLY.
options.C: A single suitability assessment performed at account opening satisfies Standard III(C) for the life of the relationship, with no obligation to update or reassess the information.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The three-part inquiry applies whenever the member is in an advisory relationship.
rationales.B:
Full statement: when in advisory relationship with client, must: (a) make reasonable inquiry into client's or prospective client's investment experience, risk and return objectives, and financial constraints PRIOR to making any investment recommendation or taking investment action and must REASSESS and update this information REGULARLY; (b) determine that investment is suitable to client's financial situation and consistent with client's written objectives, mandates, and constraints; (c) judge suitability of investments in the context of the client's total portfolio.

Correct. Per Standard III(C)(1): 'When Members and Candidates are in an advisory relationship with a client, they must: a. Make a reasonable inquiry into a client's or prospective client's investment experience, risk and return objectives, and financial constraints prior to making any investment recommendation or taking investment action and must reassess and update this information regularly. b. Determine that an investment is suitable to the client's financial situation and consistent with the client's written objectives, mandates, and constraints before making an investment recommendation or taking investment action. c. Judge the suitability of investments in the context of the client's total portfolio.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The IPS and client circumstances must be reviewed and reassessed regularly, not just at inception.

questions.stem: *Standard III(C) Suitability requirements (portfolio mandate)* *most likely*:

options.A: Managers of a fund run to a stated mandate must tailor each holding to the personal circumstances of every fund investor.
options.B: Deviation from the stated mandate is permitted whenever the manager expects it to improve returns.
options.C: when responsible for managing portfolio to specific mandate, strategy, or style, must make ONLY investment recommendations or take ONLY investment actions that are consistent with stated objectives and constraints of the portfolio.

rationales.A: Incorrect. For a mandate, strategy, or style, suitability runs to consistency with the stated objectives, not to each investor's circumstances.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Only recommendations and actions consistent with the stated mandate are permitted; expected outperformance does not authorize drift.
rationales.C: Correct. Per Standard III(C)(2): 'When Members and Candidates are responsible for managing a portfolio to a specific mandate, strategy, or style, they must make only investment recommendations or take only investment actions that are consistent with the stated objectives and constraints of the portfolio.'

questions.stem: *Standard III(D) Performance Presentation* *most likely* requires:

options.A: when communicating investment performance information, must make reasonable efforts to ensure that it is fair, accurate, and complete.
options.B: Performance presentation obligations apply only to firms that claim GIPS compliance.
options.C: Brief performance communications are exempt from the fair, accurate, and complete requirement.

rationales.A: Correct. Per Standard III(D): 'When communicating investment performance information, Members and Candidates must make reasonable efforts to ensure that it is fair, accurate, and complete.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Standard III(D) binds all members communicating performance, whether or not the firm claims GIPS compliance.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Brevity does not excuse misleading presentation; reasonable efforts at fairness, accuracy, and completeness still apply.

questions.stem: *Standard III(E) Preservation of Confidentiality* exceptions *most likely*:

options.A: Client information must be kept confidential without exception, even when disclosure is required by law.
options.B: must keep information about current, former, and prospective clients confidential UNLESS: (1) information concerns illegal activities on the part of the client or prospective client.
options.C: Confidentiality obligations end when the client relationship terminates.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Legally required disclosure is one of three exceptions, alongside illegal client activity and client permission.
rationales.B:
Full statement: must keep information about current, former, and prospective clients confidential UNLESS: (1) information concerns illegal activities on the part of the client or prospective client; (2) disclosure is required by law; or (3) the client or prospective client permits disclosure of the information.

Correct. Per Standard III(E): 'Members and Candidates must keep information about current, former, and prospective clients confidential unless: 1. The information concerns illegal activities on the part of the client or prospective client, 2. Disclosure is required by law, or 3. The client or prospective client permits disclosure of the information.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The duty covers current, former, and prospective clients.

questions.stem: *Standard IV: Duties to Employers* *most likely* subsections:

options.A: no framework.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: three subsections — IV(A) Loyalty, IV(B) Additional Compensation Arrangements, IV(C) Responsibilities of Supervisors.

rationales.A: IV(A)-(C).
rationales.B: Three subsections.
rationales.C: Correct. Per the Standards of Professional Conduct: 'iv. DUTIES TO EMPLOYERS: A. Loyalty; B. Additional Compensation Arrangements; C. Responsibilities of Supervisors.'

questions.stem: *Standard IV(A) Loyalty (to employer)* *most likely* requires:

options.A: in matters related to their employment, must act for benefit of their employer and NOT deprive their employer of the advantage of their skills and abilities, divulge confidential information, or otherwise cause harm to their employer.
options.B: Loyalty to the employer requires members to comply with directives even when they involve violating the law.
options.C: Standard IV(A) prohibits members from ever seeking employment with a competitor while still employed.

rationales.A: Correct. Per Standard IV(A): 'In matters related to their employment, Members and Candidates must act for the benefit of their employer and not deprive their employer of the advantage of their skills and abilities, divulge confidential information, or otherwise cause harm to their employer.' Solution 8: 'deprive their employer of their skills and abilities as related to their employment' is prohibited.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Employer loyalty never requires unethical or illegal action; the Code and law take precedence.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Preparing to leave, including seeking other employment, is permitted so long as employer interests are not harmed and confidential information is not misused.

questions.stem: *Standard IV(B) Additional Compensation Arrangements* *most likely* requires:

options.A: Members may accept outside compensation that conflicts with the employer's interest provided it is disclosed after the fact.
options.B: must NOT accept gifts, benefits, compensation, or consideration that competes with or might reasonably be expected to create conflict of interest with their employer's interest UNLESS they obtain WRITTEN CONSENT from ALL parties involved.
options.C: Verbal notification to a supervisor is sufficient consent for additional compensation arrangements.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Written consent from all parties involved must be obtained BEFORE accepting such arrangements.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Standard IV(B): 'Members and Candidates must not accept gifts, benefits, compensation, or consideration that competes with or might reasonably be expected to create a conflict of interest with their employer's interest unless they obtain written consent from all parties involved.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Consent must be written and from all parties involved; verbal notice is insufficient.

questions.stem: *Standard IV(C) Responsibilities of Supervisors* *most likely* requires:

options.A: A supervisor who delegates supervisory duties thereby transfers away responsibility for compliance.
options.B: Supervisors are responsible only for detecting violations after they occur, not for preventing them.
options.C: must make reasonable efforts to ensure that anyone subject to their supervision or authority complies with applicable laws, rules, regulations, and the Code and Standards.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Delegation does not relieve a supervisor of supervisory responsibility.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Reasonable efforts include prevention — establishing and enforcing procedures — not merely after-the-fact detection.
rationales.C: Correct. Per Standard IV(C): 'Members and Candidates must make reasonable efforts to ensure that anyone subject to their supervision or authority complies with applicable laws, rules, regulations, and the Code and Standards.'

questions.stem: *Standard V: Investment Analysis, Recommendations, and Actions* *most likely* subsections:

options.A: three subsections — V(A) Diligence and Reasonable Basis, V(B) Communication with Clients and Prospective Clients, V(C) Record Retention.
options.B: Standard V contains two subsections: Diligence and Reasonable Basis, and Record Retention.
options.C: Standard V includes a subsection governing Referral Fees.

rationales.A: Correct. Per the Standards of Professional Conduct: 'v. INVESTMENT ANALYSIS, RECOMMENDATIONS, AND ACTIONS: A. Diligence and Reasonable Basis; B. Communication with Clients and Prospective Clients; C. Record Retention.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. There are three subsections; Communication with Clients and Prospective Clients is V(B).
rationales.C: Incorrect. Referral Fees is Standard VI(C), under Conflicts of Interest.

questions.stem: *Standard V(A) Diligence and Reasonable Basis* *most likely* requires:

options.A: Diligence obligations can be fully satisfied by relying on the sponsor's marketing materials for a recommended product.
options.B: must: (1) exercise diligence, independence, and thoroughness in analyzing investments, making investment recommendations, and taking investment actions.
options.C: Standard V(A) requires a reasonable basis for sell recommendations only, not for purchases.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Diligence, independence, and thoroughness require analysis beyond promotional material.
rationales.B:
Full statement: must: (1) exercise diligence, independence, and thoroughness in analyzing investments, making investment recommendations, and taking investment actions; (2) have reasonable and adequate basis, supported by appropriate research and investigation, for any investment analysis, recommendation, or action.

Correct. Per Standard V(A): 'Members and Candidates must: 1. Exercise diligence, independence, and thoroughness in analyzing investments, making investment recommendations, and taking investment actions. 2. Have a reasonable and adequate basis, supported by appropriate research and investigation, for any investment analysis, recommendation, or action.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The requirement applies to all investment analysis, recommendations, and actions, in both directions.

questions.stem: *Standard V(B) Communication with Clients* *most likely* requires (services/costs):

options.A: Disclosure of costs is required only upon a client's written request.
options.B: The nature of services must be described only in the initial engagement letter and never updated.
options.C: must disclose to clients and prospective clients the nature of services provided along with information about costs to client associated with those services.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The nature of services and associated client costs must be affirmatively disclosed.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Disclosure obligations are ongoing, not confined to the initial engagement document.
rationales.C: Correct. Per Standard V(B)(1): 'Disclose to clients and prospective clients the nature of the services provided, along with information about the costs to the client associated with those services.'

questions.stem: *Standard V(B)(2) Communication — investment process* *most likely* requires:

options.A: must disclose to clients and prospective clients basic format and general principles of investment processes they use to analyze investments, select securities, and construct portfolios and must PROMPTLY DISCLOSE any changes that might materially affect those processes.
options.B: Members must disclose the proprietary details and full model code of their investment process.
options.C: Disclosure of the investment process is required only for quantitative strategies.

rationales.A: Correct. Per Standard V(B)(2): 'Disclose to clients and prospective clients the basic format and general principles of the investment processes they use to analyze investments, select securities, and construct portfolios and must promptly disclose any changes that might materially affect those processes.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The requirement is the basic format and general principles of the process, not proprietary detail.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The disclosure obligation applies to whatever process is used, quantitative or fundamental.

questions.stem: *Standard V(B)(3) Communication — limitations and risks* *most likely* requires:

options.A: Only risks that have historically materialized must be disclosed to clients.
options.B: must disclose to clients and prospective clients significant limitations and risks associated with the investment process.
options.C: Limitations of the investment process may be omitted if they are described in the fund's offering documents.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Significant limitations and risks of the process must be disclosed, not just realized ones.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Standard V(B)(3): 'Disclose to clients and prospective clients significant limitations and risks associated with the investment process.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The member's disclosure duty to clients is not discharged by references buried in offering documents.

questions.stem: *Standard V(B)(4) Communication — important factors* *most likely*:

options.A: All factors considered in the analysis must be included in client communications, regardless of importance.
options.B: Factor selection for communications may be driven by which factors best support the recommendation.
options.C: use reasonable judgment in identifying which factors are important to their investment analyses, recommendations, or actions and include those factors in communications with clients and prospective clients.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Reasonable judgment identifies the IMPORTANT factors; exhaustive inclusion is not required.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Selecting only supportive factors would mislead; judgment must be exercised objectively.
rationales.C: Correct. Per Standard V(B)(4): 'Use reasonable judgment in identifying which factors are important to their investment analyses, recommendations, or actions and include those factors in communications with clients and prospective clients.'

questions.stem: *Standard V(B)(5) Communication — fact vs opinion* *most likely* requires:

options.A: distinguish between fact and opinion in the presentation of investment analysis and recommendations.
options.B: no requirement.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Correct. Per Standard V(B)(5): 'Distinguish between fact and opinion in the presentation of investment analysis and recommendations.'
rationales.B: Fact vs opinion.
rationales.C: Fact/opinion.

questions.stem: *Standard V(C) Record Retention* *most likely* requires:

options.A: Records supporting recommendations may be discarded once the recommendation is communicated to clients.
options.B: must develop and maintain appropriate records to support their investment analyses, recommendations, actions, and other investment-related communications with clients and prospective clients.
options.C: Record retention is solely the firm's obligation and imposes no responsibility on individual members.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Records must be developed and MAINTAINED to support analyses, recommendations, actions, and communications.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Standard V(C): 'Members and Candidates must develop and maintain appropriate records to support their investment analyses, recommendations, actions, and other investment-related communications with clients and prospective clients.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The develop-and-maintain obligation is placed on members and candidates themselves.

questions.stem: *Standard VI: Conflicts of Interest* *most likely* subsections:

options.A: no framework.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: three subsections — VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts; VI(B) Priority of Transactions; VI(C) Referral Fees.

rationales.A: VI(A)-(C).
rationales.B: Three subsections.
rationales.C: Correct. Per the Standards of Professional Conduct: 'vi. CONFLICTS OF INTEREST: A. Avoid or Disclose Conflicts; B. Priority of Transactions; C. Referral Fees.'

questions.stem: *Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts* *most likely* requires:

options.A: must AVOID or make full and fair disclosure of all matters that could reasonably be expected to impair their independence and objectivity and interfere with respective duties to their clients, prospective clients, and employer.
options.B: Conflicts of interest need only be disclosed internally to compliance, not to clients or other affected parties.
options.C: Disclosure is required only for conflicts that have already impaired objectivity, not potential conflicts.

rationales.A:
Full statement: must AVOID or make full and fair disclosure of all matters that could reasonably be expected to impair their independence and objectivity and interfere with respective duties to their clients, prospective clients, and employer; must ensure that such disclosures are prominent, are delivered in plain language, and communicate the relevant information effectively.

Correct. Per Standard VI(A): 'Members and Candidates must avoid or make full and fair disclosure of all matters that could reasonably be expected to impair their independence and objectivity and interfere with respective duties to their clients, prospective clients, and employer. Members and Candidates must ensure that such disclosures are prominent, are delivered in plain language, and communicate the relevant information effectively.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Full and fair disclosure must reach the parties whose interests could be affected — clients, prospects, and employer as relevant.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Matters that COULD REASONABLY BE EXPECTED to impair independence and objectivity are covered — potential conflicts included.

questions.stem: *Standard VI(B) Priority of Transactions* *most likely* requires:

options.A: Personal transactions may be executed simultaneously with client orders provided prices are identical.
options.B: investment transactions for clients and employers must have PRIORITY over investment transactions in which a Member or Candidate is the beneficial owner.
options.C: Standard VI(B) prohibits members from ever trading securities held in client accounts.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Client and employer transactions take priority; simultaneous personal execution can still disadvantage clients.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Standard VI(B): 'Investment transactions for clients and employers must have priority over investment transactions in which a Member or Candidate is the beneficial owner.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Personal investment in the same securities is permitted once clients and employers have had priority.

questions.stem: *Standard VI(C) Referral Fees* *most likely* requires:

options.A: Referral fees must be disclosed only when the referred client asks about compensation arrangements.
options.B: Referral fee disclosure applies to cash payments but not to soft benefits or in-kind consideration.
options.C: must disclose to their employer, clients, and prospective clients, as appropriate, any compensation, consideration, or benefit received from or paid to others for the recommendation of products or services.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Disclosure must be made affirmatively to employer, clients, and prospective clients as appropriate.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Any compensation, consideration, or BENEFIT received or paid for recommendations is covered.
rationales.C: Correct. Per Standard VI(C): 'Members and Candidates must disclose to their employer, clients, and prospective clients, as appropriate, any compensation, consideration, or benefit received from or paid to others for the recommendation of products or services.'

questions.stem: *Standard VII: Responsibilities as CFA Institute Member or CFA Candidate* *most likely* subsections:

options.A: two subsections — VII(A) Conduct as Participants in CFA Institute Programs and VII(B) Reference to CFA Institute, the CFA Designation, and the CFA Program.
options.B: Standard VII contains a single subsection addressing conduct during CFA examinations.
options.C: Standard VII includes a subsection on Performance Presentation.

rationales.A: Correct. Per the Standards of Professional Conduct: 'vii. RESPONSIBILITIES AS A CFA INSTITUTE MEMBER OR CFA CANDIDATE: A. Conduct as Participants in CFA Institute Programs; B. Reference to CFA Institute, the CFA Designation, and the CFA Program.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. There are two subsections: VII(A) program conduct and VII(B) reference to the Institute, designation, and program.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Performance Presentation is Standard III(D).

questions.stem: *Standard VII(A) Conduct as Participants in CFA Institute Programs* *most likely*:

options.A: VII(A) restricts only the disclosure of live exam questions, not other program-integrity conduct.
options.B: must NOT engage in any conduct that compromises the reputation or integrity of CFA Institute or the CFA designation or the integrity, validity, or security of CFA Institute programs.
options.C: Expressing opinions critical of CFA Institute policy violates Standard VII(A).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Any conduct compromising the reputation, integrity, validity, or security of Institute programs is covered.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Standard VII(A): 'Members and Candidates must not engage in any conduct that compromises the reputation or integrity of CFA Institute or the CFA designation or the integrity, validity, or security of CFA Institute programs.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Members may disagree with and criticize Institute policies; the standard targets integrity-compromising conduct, not opinions.

questions.stem: *Standard VII(B) Reference to CFA Institute, Designation, Program* *most likely*:

options.A: Members may represent that holding the CFA charter guarantees superior investment performance, since the designation certifies advanced competence in investment management.
options.B: A candidate who has registered for Level III may append 'CFA, expected 2027' to their name, because imminent completion of the Program qualifies as candidacy disclosure.
options.C: when referring to CFA Institute, CFA Institute membership, the CFA designation, or candidacy in the CFA Program, Members and Candidates must NOT misrepresent or exaggerate the meaning or implications of membership in CFA Institute, holding the CFA designation, or candidacy in the CFA Program.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Implying performance superiority misrepresents the meaning of the designation.
rationales.B: Incorrect. There is no partial designation; candidacy may be stated only factually (e.g., 'passed Level II; registered for the Level III exam').
rationales.C: Correct. Per Standard VII(B): 'When referring to CFA Institute, CFA Institute membership, the CFA designation, or candidacy in the CFA Program, Members and Candidates must not misrepresent or exaggerate the meaning or implications of membership in CFA Institute, holding the CFA designation, or candidacy in the CFA Program.' Per Solution 10: Standard VII explicitly addresses 'reference to the CFA Program.'

questions.stem: *Number of top-level Standards* *most likely*:

options.A: seven top-level Standards: I. Professionalism.
options.B: six.
options.C: eight.

rationales.A:
Full statement: seven top-level Standards: I. Professionalism; II. Integrity of Capital Markets; III. Duties to Clients; IV. Duties to Employers; V. Investment Analysis, Recommendations, and Actions; VI. Conflicts of Interest; VII. Responsibilities as a CFA Institute Member or CFA Candidate.

Correct. Per the Standards of Professional Conduct there are seven top-level Standards: 'i. PROFESSIONALISM; ii. INTEGRITY OF CAPITAL MARKETS; iii. DUTIES TO CLIENTS; iv. DUTIES TO EMPLOYERS; v. INVESTMENT ANALYSIS, RECOMMENDATIONS, AND ACTIONS; vi. CONFLICTS OF INTEREST; vii. RESPONSIBILITIES AS A CFA INSTITUTE MEMBER OR CFA CANDIDATE.'
rationales.B: Seven Standards.
rationales.C: Seven.

questions.stem: *Best strict-law approach for I(A)* when conflict exists *most likely*:

options.A: In a conflict of laws, members should comply with the law of their country of residence.
options.B: in event of conflict between applicable laws, rules, or regulations, must comply with the MORE STRICT law, rule, or regulation.
options.C: In a conflict of laws, the less strict law governs so long as local regulators permit it.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Residence does not decide the question; the MORE STRICT applicable rule governs.
rationales.B:
Full statement: in event of conflict between applicable laws, rules, or regulations, must comply with the MORE STRICT law, rule, or regulation; principle applies whenever different jurisdictions or professional bodies have overlapping but inconsistent requirements — Member/Candidate must follow the higher standard.

Correct. Per Standard I(A): 'In the event of conflict, Members and Candidates must comply with the more strict law, rule, or regulation.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The stricter of the applicable laws, rules, or regulations must be followed.

questions.stem: *I(A) dissociation obligation* *most likely*:

options.A: Members satisfy Standard I(A) by merely abstaining from personally initiating the violation while continuing to work on the account.
options.B: Dissociation is required only after a regulator has formally charged the wrongdoer.
options.C: must NOT knowingly participate or assist in and must DISSOCIATE from any violation of applicable laws, rules, or regulations.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Knowing participation OR assistance is prohibited; continued association with the activity can itself constitute participation.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The dissociation duty arises from knowledge of the violation, not from regulatory action.
rationales.C:
Full statement: must NOT knowingly participate or assist in and must DISSOCIATE from any violation of applicable laws, rules, or regulations; dissociation means separating from the violation — could include refusing to participate, reporting concerns, or leaving position if necessary.

Correct. Per Standard I(A): 'Members and Candidates must not knowingly participate or assist in and must dissociate from any violation of such laws, rules, or regulations.'

questions.stem: *Reassessment obligation for suitability* under III(C) *most likely*:

options.A: in advisory relationship, must reasonably inquire into client's investment experience, risk and return objectives, and financial constraints PRIOR to making any investment recommendation or taking investment action AND must reassess and update this information REGULARLY.
options.B: Suitability information gathered at the start of an advisory relationship remains valid without update.
options.C: Reassessment of client circumstances is required only when the client requests it.

rationales.A:
Full statement: in advisory relationship, must reasonably inquire into client's investment experience, risk and return objectives, and financial constraints PRIOR to making any investment recommendation or taking investment action AND must reassess and update this information REGULARLY; not a one-time event but ongoing obligation.

Correct. Per Standard III(C)(1)(a): '[Members and Candidates must] Make a reasonable inquiry into a client's or prospective client's investment experience, risk and return objectives, and financial constraints prior to making any investment recommendation or taking investment action and must reassess and update this information regularly.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Information must be gathered PRIOR to acting and reassessed and updated regularly.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Regular reassessment is the member's affirmative duty, not a client-initiated option.

questions.stem: *Suitability in context of total portfolio* under III(C) *most likely*:

options.A: Suitability must be judged security by security, with each holding individually matching the client's risk profile.
options.B: in advisory relationship must judge the suitability of investments in the context of the client's TOTAL PORTFOLIO — not just in isolation.
options.C: Portfolio context matters only for concentrated positions above 10% of client assets.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Suitability is judged in the context of the client's TOTAL portfolio; an individually risky holding can be suitable within a diversified whole.
rationales.B:
